drills.scenario: FitForge, a local gym, is brand new to Meta ads. The owner has never run a campaign, has no landing page, no conversion pixel, and no funnel built. He wants to start learning the platform this week without risking much money on broken tracking.
drills.question: Given the Flight Plan philosophy of mastering fundamentals first, what is the smartest first move?
options: Build a Stage Three video + retargeting funnel with Dynamic Product Ads so it's future-proof
Run a low-risk Like Campaign / boosted post to get hands-on practice and real audience data without needing landing pages, thank-you pages, or pixels
Hire an agency to set up a full conversion campaign before he understands anything
Spend two weeks mastering every dial in Ads Manager and Power Editor before launching
drills.explanation: Flight Plan: you start in a single-engine Cessna with an instructor, not a Boeing 747. A Like Campaign is the beginner's 'flight simulator' — low-risk learning with no landing pages, pixels, or tracking required, while still yielding real data (interest performance, placements, audience reactions). The book also says 'just start' and get quick momentum before optimizing. Stage Three requires 1,000+ conversions first, so option A is far too advanced.

drills.scenario: A plumber crushed it on Google Ads with the headline 'We fix broken water pipes — there in 30 minutes.' He copies that exact ad onto Facebook targeting homeowners in his city. After two weeks it has terrible CTR and zero jobs booked.
drills.question: What is the core reason the ad bombed on Facebook?
options: His budget was too low to exit the learning phase
Facebook is interruption marketing — no one there is searching for a plumber; a direct-intent solution ad that works on Google (active intent) falls flat
His targeting interests were too broad
He should have used a carousel instead of a single image
drills.explanation: Intent vs. Interruption: Google/YouTube searchers show active intent so direct solution ads work. Facebook is interruption marketing — people want an escape, not a plumber, so the same ad bombs. AdWords skills don't carry over. The fix is messaging that meets people where they are on the awareness staircase, not tighter targeting or more budget.

drills.scenario: A nutrition coach sells an Intermittent Fasting course. To get easy early sales she targets ONLY people who already follow well-known IF influencers and know exactly what intermittent fasting is. Sales come quickly at first, but within a few weeks volume stalls and she can't scale.
drills.question: Using UPSYD, what went wrong and what should she do?
options: She targeted only 'Your Solution Aware' people — a small audience that yields easy early sales but can't sustain a business; she should start around Level 3 (Solution Aware) and expand outward
She targeted Unaware people who will never buy; she should narrow to only Deal-stage buyers
Her offer is the problem, so awareness level is irrelevant
She should pour 90% of budget into retargeting to fix the stall
drills.explanation: UPSYD (Unaware, Problem Aware, Solution Aware, Your Solution Aware, Deal). Targeting only 'Your Solution Aware' people gets sales easily but the audience is too small to sustain or scale a business. The book's guidance: begin at Level 3 (Solution Aware), build momentum, then expand outward, perpetually moving people up the staircase.

drills.scenario: Two violin teachers are twins. Ricky's ads show off his 10,000 hours of mastery, lean on referrals and discounts, and frame violin as a daunting climb only the talented can make — he gets admiration but almost no sign-ups. James teaches a quick shareable win, lets prospects DO it and succeed, frames mastery as achievable shortcuts, and offers a low-friction lead magnet — he has a mile-long waiting list.
drills.question: What is the central lesson, and which approach should a new advertiser copy?
options: Ricky's — credibility and proof of expertise are what convert cold audiences
James's — make the audience the hero and the prospect feel capable, not impress them with how great YOU are
Neither; discounts are the only reliable conversion lever
Ricky's, because referrals are the cheapest acquisition channel
drills.explanation: Audience-First (James) vs You-First (Ricky): make the audience the star/hero, not yourself. James lets the prospect succeed and feel capable, framing mastery as achievable shortcuts with a low-friction lead magnet. Ricky showcases his own mastery and gets admiration but zero conversions. 'He wasn't the star of the show — his audience was.' Be like James.

drills.scenario: A Shopify pet brand sells a $70 bottle of joint-support powder for dogs. The founder saw an info-marketer's funnel (cold ad → opt-in for a free PDF → email sequence → sell a course) crush it, so she copies that exact funnel structure to sell her physical product. It converts poorly.
drills.question: What's the diagnosis?
options: She picked the wrong campaign objective; switching to Reach will fix it

There's no one-size-fits-all funnel — she copied a model built for information marketers; she should identify her Business Category (eCommerce/Physical Products) and use that category's proven selling system
Her product is overpriced and must be cut to $20
She needs more retargeting; cold traffic never buys physical products
drills.explanation: Three Business Categories: each needs a DIFFERENT selling system because they differ in offer, margins, purchase frequency, and trust required. The big mistake is copying a model built specifically for information marketers — with interruption marketing there is no one-size-fits-all funnel. Identify your category (here, eCommerce/Physical Products), use its proven system, then tweak.

drills.scenario: You're a strategist onboarding a new client — a meal-prep service — and you want to run the pre-flight diagnostics in the correct sequence before building any campaign.
drills.question: Put the pre-flight diagnostic steps in the correct order.
options: Identify which of the three Business Categories the client falls into and adopt that category's proven selling system
Map the target audience across the UPSYD awareness levels
Decide where on the UPSYD staircase to start (default: Level 3, Solution Aware)
Match the value/trust in the ad to the size of the ask
Ask 'How is this person coming into our sales funnel?' (guard up vs guard down)
Run the 7 Power Questions on the copy before finishing the ad
drills.explanation: This is the book's Pre-Flight Diagnostics checklist order: (1) pick the Business Category and its selling system, (2) map the audience on UPSYD, (3) choose where on the staircase to start (default Level 3), (4) match value/trust to the size of the ask, (5) ask how they enter the funnel (guard up vs down), and finally (6) run the 7 Power Questions on the copy before finishing.

drills.scenario: A course creator wants to sell a 90-minute live webinar (a fairly big ask) to cold traffic. He's debating between (a) a simple single-image link post with one line of short copy and a 'Register now' button, or (b) a short video clip that pre-commits the viewer paired with long copy that builds the case.
drills.question: Which entry matches the size of the ask, and why?
options: Option A — short copy always wins on cold traffic because attention spans are short
Option B — a webinar is a bigger ask, so pre-commit with a short clip plus long copy; matching value/trust to the size of the ask brings them in guard-down
Either works equally; the ask size doesn't affect the creative
Option A, because video is only for Stage Three after 1,000 conversions
drills.explanation: Match value/trust to the size of the ask. A minor ask (cheat-sheet opt-in) needs only a simple image + short copy. A bigger ask like a 90–120 minute webinar should pre-commit the prospect with a short clip + long copy. This also relates to guard-up vs guard-down: video-first leads arrive pre-sold rather than expecting to be sold.

drills.scenario: A bootstrapped Shopify candle brand runs image-only direct-response ads. A new winner spikes for about a week then dies; she relaunches a fresh image, it spikes and dies again, and she's exhausted by the constant rotation. She wants a 'set it and forget it' campaign that runs profitably for months.
drills.question: What does the module recommend she shift toward?
options: Run more image variations faster — the sprint model just needs higher velocity
Move toward long-term video ads, which run profitably for 6+ months ('the steps that make up the marathon'); image ads are a sprint that dies in ~7–10 days and only works on already-primed buyers
Switch entirely to retargeting since cold image ads can't last
Raise production budget for a Hollywood-grade image ad
drills.explanation: Long-term video ads beat short-term image/direct-response ads. Image ads are a 'sprint' that only works on already-primed buyers and dies in ~7–10 days ('a race for fools'). Video ads are 'the steps that make up the marathon' and can run profitably for 6 months to 1–2 years, enabling a 'set it and forget it' campaign — and they don't require high production value (an iPhone is fine).

drills.scenario: A founder asks you to settle a debate on her team about where the real leverage is in Meta advertising when starting out. People are arguing over hyper-targeting, retargeting stacks, and mastering every dial in Ads Manager.
drills.question: According to the module, which statements about leverage and priorities are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)
options: Getting the offer/messaging right is THE key — without a winning offer you can't scale no matter how good a 'Facebook ninja' you are
Over-focusing on targeting data and hyper-targeting/retargeting is one of the biggest mistakes; strategy and messaging win
Profitable campaigns typically run roughly 90% cold traffic and 10% retargeting — retargeting is 'icing on top'
Mastering every dial in Ads Manager/Power Editor is the highest-leverage skill for beginners
Selling to the RIGHT PERSON beats all sales, copy, and negotiation techniques
drills.explanation: The module repeatedly stresses that strategy, offer, and messaging are the high-leverage 'small hinges that swing big doors' — not platform mechanics. Getting the offer/messaging right is THE key; over-focusing on targeting/retargeting is the biggest mistake; profitable campaigns are ~90% cold / ~10% retargeting; and per Perry Marshall, selling to the right person beats all techniques. Option D is explicitly the wrong focus: 'don't live inside Ads Manager when starting.'

drills.scenario: A SaaS founder is impatient. She's run ads for three weeks, hasn't hit profitability, and wants to know if she's hit the 'easy' part yet. She's generated about 80 conversions so far.
drills.question: Per the Flight Plan milestones, what's the accurate read on her situation?
options: She's past the hard part; profitability should now be automatic

Generating the first 100+ conversions is the hardest part of the whole journey (like a shuttle launch before booster separation); she's almost through it, and it gets easier after — but these counts are guidelines, not rigid rules
She must hit 1,000 conversions before anything gets easier
Conversion counts are irrelevant; only CTR matters
drills.explanation: Milestone note: generating your first 100+ conversions is the hardest part of the whole journey — like the first two minutes of a shuttle launch before booster separation — and once past it the path gets easier. At ~80 she's almost there. Conversion counts are explicitly guidelines, not rigid rules. (Stage Three, the 1,000-conversion threshold, comes much later.)
